source_paper_title: Improved Guarantees for Offline Stochastic Matching via New Ordered Contention Resolution Schemes
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Brubach_2025, title={Improved Guarantees for Offline Stochastic Matching via New Ordered Contention Resolution Schemes}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0256}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0256}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Brubach, Brian and Grammel, Nathaniel and Ma, Will and Srinivasan, Aravind}, year={2025}, month=nov, pages={3243–3256} }
context:
Page 6, end of Subsection 1.2 ("Reinterpretation as contention resolution under negative correlation")

We leave it as an open question whether a \((1 - 1/e)\)-selectable Random-order Contention Resolution Scheme is possible under property (2).
title: Random-order contention resolution for rank-1 matroids with negative correlation
background_latex:
A rank-1 matroid can be represented by a ground set of elements (here, edges) \(E\) with the feasibility constraint that at most one element may be selected. Equivalently, in a star graph with center vertex \(v\), the feasible matchings choose at most one edge incident to \(v\).

An ordered (or random-order) contention resolution scheme (CRS) for this rank-1 constraint operates as follows. Each element \(e\in E\) has an initially unknown activeness indicator \(Z_e\in\{0,1\}\). The algorithm observes elements one-by-one in some order; when an element \(e\) is revealed to be active (\(Z_e=1\)) and no previously accepted element has been chosen, the algorithm may either accept \(e\) (and then must reject all later elements) or irrevocably discard \(e\).

A random-order CRS means the observation order is a uniformly random permutation of \(E\).

Let \(z_e := \Pr[Z_e=1]\) denote the marginal activeness probability of element \(e\). The rank-1 feasibility region corresponding to these marginals is \(\sum_{e\in E} z_e \le 1\) (and \(0\le z_e\le 1\)).

A CRS is called \(c\)-selectable if for every instance and every element \(e\), the ex ante probability that the algorithm accepts \(e\) is at least \(c\, z_e\).

In the setting of this paper, the activeness indicators \((Z_e)_{e\in E}\) need not be independent; they satisfy a (pairwise-to-setwise) negative correlation property: for every subset \(S\subseteq E\) and \(b\in\{0,1\}\),
\[\Pr\big[\bigwedge_{e\in S}(Z_e=b)\big] \le \prod_{e\in S} \Pr[Z_e=b].\]
In particular, the case \(b=0\) asserts that joint inactivity events are no more likely than under independence.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether there exists a \((1-1/e)\)-selectable random-order contention resolution scheme for a rank-1 matroid when the activeness indicators \((Z_e)_{e\in E}\) satisfy the negative correlation property
\[\Pr\big[\bigwedge_{e\in S}(Z_e=b)\big] \le \prod_{e\in S} \Pr[Z_e=b]\quad \forall S\subseteq E,\ b\in\{0,1\},\]
with given marginals \(z_e=\Pr[Z_e=1]\) obeying \(\sum_{e\in E} z_e \le 1\). That is, decide whether one can guarantee for all such instances that each element \(e\) is accepted with probability at least \((1-1/e) z_e\) when elements are revealed in a uniformly random order and decisions are irrevocable.

Each element \(e\in E\) has an initially unknown activeness indicator \(Z_e\in\{0,1\}\). The algorithm observes elements one-by-one in some order; when an element \(e\) is revealed to be active (\(Z_e=1\)) and no previously accepted element has been chosen, the algorithm may either accept \(e\) (and then must reject all later elements) or irrevocably discard \(e\).

Let \(z_e := \Pr[Z_e=1]\) denote the marginal activeness probability of element \(e\). The rank-1 feasibility region corresponding to these marginals is \(\sum_{e\in E} z_e \le 1\) (and \(0\le z_e\le 1\)).

\[\Pr\big[\bigwedge_{e\in S}(Z_e=b)\big] \le \prod_{e\in S} \Pr[Z_e=b]\quad \forall S\subseteq E,\ b\in\{0,1\},\]

location_in_paper: Page 6, end of Subsection 1.2 ("Reinterpretation as contention resolution under negative correlation")
open_problem_quote: We leave it as an open question whether a \((1 - 1/e)\)-selectable Random-order Contention Resolution Scheme is possible under property (2).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can computationally search for small negatively correlated distributions over \((Z_e)\) and candidate random-order acceptance rules to upper-bound achievable selectability, but encoding all negatively correlated distributions is nontrivial and quickly becomes large. Still, small-star instances may reveal barriers or suggest tight examples.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate schemes for rank-1 in random order can be parameterized (e.g., acceptance thresholds based on position in the order and/or revealed activeness history), and their worst-case selectability can be estimated by simulation over adversarially generated negatively correlated distributions satisfying the constraint \(\sum z_e\le 1\). This gives an automatic fitness signal (empirical minimum ratio of acceptance probability to \(z_e\)) suitable for iterative search.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem asks whether the classical \\(1-1/e\\) selectability for rank-1 ("choose at most one") contention resolution in random order—known under independent activeness—extends to the substantially more delicate setting where activeness indicators satisfy strong negative correlation bounds for both \\(b=0\\) and \\(b=1\\). In the source paper, negative correlation arises naturally from dependent rounding (Gandhi--Khuller--Parthasarathy--Srinivasan), and the authors show that when the algorithm may choose the order (an ordered CRS), sorting by weight achieves \\(1-1/e\\) in expectation even though per-element guarantees can fail for correlation-agnostic schemes. They explicitly leave open whether random order can still guarantee \\(1-1/e\\) per element.

Prior work of Lee and Singla establishes \\(1-1/e\\)-selectable random-order/online contention resolution for rank-1 matroids under independence, via ex-ante prophet inequalities, while Adamczyk and W\l{}odarczyk develop broader random-order CRS theory with constant factors. These results do not directly apply under negative correlation, and the source paper provides evidence of a barrier: under certain negatively correlated constructions, any scheme that ignores correlation structure can degrade to \\(1/2\\)-selectability for individual elements. At present (given no forward-citation evidence supplied), there is no documented resolution of whether a \\(1-1/e\\)-selectable random-order CRS exists under the stated negative correlation property; the problem therefore appears to remain open.

Promising directions include: (i) extending ex-ante prophet inequality techniques to distributions with negative dependence (possibly requiring new martingale or correlation-gap style bounds tailored to random arrival), (ii) identifying additional structural assumptions beyond (pairwise-to-setwise) negative correlation that are satisfied by dependent rounding outputs and are sufficient for \\(1-1/e\\) in random order, or (iii) proving an impossibility result by constructing a negatively correlated family (respecting the \\(b\in\{0,1\}\\) inequalities) that forces any random-order stopping rule to lose a constant factor below \\(1-1/e\\).

sources.title: Optimal online contention resolution schemes via ex-ante prophet inequalities
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces and analyzes random-order/online contention resolution schemes and shows a \\(1-1/e\\)-selectable random-order CRS for rank-1 matroids under independent activeness, via an ex-ante prophet inequality framework.
sources.connection: Provides the baseline \\(1-1/e\\) guarantee in the independent case and the conceptual framework (ex-ante prophet inequalities) that the open problem asks to extend to negatively correlated activeness.

sources.title: Random order contention resolution schemes
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops general theory of random-order contention resolution schemes, primarily for matroid-type constraints, giving constant-factor selectability guarantees in random order.
sources.connection: Supplies general random-order CRS machinery and benchmarks; helps situate what is known beyond rank-1 and highlights where correlation assumptions enter.

sources.title: Dependent rounding and its applications to approximation algorithms
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces dependent rounding with negative correlation properties: for rounded indicators \\(Z_e\\), joint events of the form \\(\wedge_{e\in S}(Z_e=b)\\) are upper-bounded by the product of marginals.
sources.connection: This is the canonical source of the negative correlation property assumed in the open problem; it motivates why such correlations arise and what inequalities are available.

sources.title: Attenuate locally, win globally: Attenuation-based frameworks for online stochastic matching with timeouts
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops attenuation-based frameworks for online/offline stochastic matching with timeouts, yielding contention-resolution-like guarantees under certain models.
sources.connection: Provides techniques (attenuation, random-order analysis) that influence the 2022 paper and may be adaptable when studying rank-1 random-order CRS under correlation.

sources.title: Improved Guarantees for Offline Stochastic Matching via new Ordered Contention Resolution Schemes
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives a \\(1-1/e\\)-selectable ordered CRS for rank-1 matroids under the negative correlation property when the algorithm may choose the order; leaves open whether the same is possible in random order.
sources.connection: This is the originating paper that formulates the open question: extend \\(1-1/e\\) selectability from ordered (chosen order) to random-order under negative correlation.
